criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain what happens in my body during a fight, flight, or freeze response.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot describe stress response changes
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Names one or two body changes during stress with prompting
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes some body changes but may confuse response types
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly explains physical changes in fight, flight, or freeze responses with accurate examples
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains stress responses and can teach or demonstrate understanding to others

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents one distinct physiological concept that requires understanding the body's acute stress response mechanisms.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe the three parts of the habit loop.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify habit loop parts
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Names one or two habit loop components with prompting
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Identifies most habit loop parts but may miss connections
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Accurately describes all three parts of the habit loop and their relationships
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes habit loop components and applies framework to analyze specific behaviors

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents a separate behavioral mechanism concept distinct from stress physiology, requiring understanding of cue-routine-reward cycles.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain how the amygdala and prefrontal cortex interact during a stressful moment.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot explain brain region interactions during stress.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Names amygdala and prefrontal cortex but unclear about their interaction.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Explains basic roles but interaction description lacks detail or accuracy.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly explains how amygdala triggers stress response and prefrontal cortex regulates it during stressful moments.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains interaction and can give specific examples of this process.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents understanding a specific neurobiological mechanism requiring distinct knowledge of brain anatomy and stress physiology.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain what neuroplasticity means.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot define or explain neuroplasticity.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Mentions brain change but explanation is vague or incorrect.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Basic understanding that brain can change but lacks detail.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly explains neuroplasticity as the brain's ability to reorganize and form new connections.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains concept and connects it to learning or habit formation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Neuroplasticity is a distinct foundational concept separate from stress mechanisms, requiring separate assessment of this core neuroscience principle.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe how the habit loop explains behaviour change at the level of mechanism.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot connect habit loop to behaviour change.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Recalls habit loop parts but unclear about behaviour change mechanism.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes habit loop and mentions behaviour change but lacks mechanistic detail.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly describes how cue-routine-reward cycle creates automatic behaviours and enables behaviour change through modification.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes mechanism and can apply it to specific behaviour change scenarios.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This requires distinct understanding of how habit formation works mechanistically, building on previous band knowledge but requiring deeper mechanistic explanation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can use accurate neuroscience vocabulary to explain the interactions between stress, emotion, attention, and habit as an integrated system.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No neuroscience vocabulary used or system connections explained.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Uses some neuroscience terms with prompting to describe isolated components.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Uses neuroscience vocabulary to explain some connections between stress, emotion, attention and habit.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Accurately uses neuroscience vocabulary to explain how stress, emotion, attention and habit interact as an integrated system.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains system interactions with precise vocabulary and can teach others these concepts.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents a complex understanding (U-level) requiring integration of multiple neurobiological processes into a coherent systems explanation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe the role of the HPA axis in the stress response mechanism.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify or describe HPA axis components.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Names some HPA axis components with prompting but unclear on function.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes basic HPA axis components and their general role in stress response.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Accurately describes the HPA axis pathway and explains its specific role in coordinating stress responses.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes HPA axis function and connects it to broader physiological regulation systems.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a distinct factual knowledge (K-level) criterion about a specific neurobiological pathway that can be assessed independently from the systems understanding.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify whether a health claim comes from a trusted adult or source.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot distinguish between trusted and untrusted sources.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies trusted sources occasionally with adult prompting and support.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Identifies trusted sources sometimes, showing inconsistent recognition patterns.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently identifies whether health claims come from trusted adults or reliable sources.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Identifies trusted sources consistently and can teach others about source reliability.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because identifying trusted sources and explaining reasoning are distinct cognitive operations at different levels - recognition versus articulation of thinking process.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain my reasoning for trusting or not trusting a health information source.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot explain reasoning about source trustworthiness.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Explains reasoning occasionally with prompting, often using simple statements.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Explains reasoning sometimes independently, using basic justifications for trust decisions.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently explains clear reasoning for why they trust or don't trust health information sources.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains detailed reasoning and can help others understand source evaluation thinking.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because identifying trusted sources and explaining reasoning are distinct cognitive operations at different levels - recognition versus articulation of thinking process.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare two health information sources by identifying key differences between them.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify differences between health sources.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies basic differences with prompting and support.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes identifies key differences between sources independently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently identifies and clearly describes key differences between health information sources.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Systematically compares sources and explains comparison approach to others.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This targets the comparison cognitive operation, which requires students to systematically examine and contrast two sources before making trust judgements.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe which health information source I trust more and explain my reasoning.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot explain trust preferences for health sources.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: States preference but reasoning unclear or prompted.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes explains trust reasoning with basic justification.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly describes preferred source and provides logical reasoning for trust decision.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Articulates nuanced reasoning and helps others evaluate source trustworthiness.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This targets the evaluation and justification cognitive operation, which is distinct from comparison and requires students to make reasoned judgements about trustworthiness.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can examine and categorise the type of evidence supporting a health claim.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify evidence types in health claims.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies basic evidence types with prompting and support.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes categorises evidence types but may miss key distinctions.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently examines and accurately categorises different types of evidence in health claims.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Categorises evidence types and explains their relative strengths and limitations.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This addresses the first cognitive operation of examining and analysing evidence types, which requires specific analytical skills distinct from bias detection.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify potential sources of bias in health information providers.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify bias in health information sources.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies obvious bias with prompting and guidance.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes recognises potential bias but reasoning may be incomplete.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently identifies whether sources have reasons to be biased and explains those reasons.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Identifies bias and explains how it might influence the information presented.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This addresses the second cognitive operation of identifying bias, which involves different analytical skills from evidence evaluation and can be assessed independently.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can compare the quality of evidence supporting different sides of a contested health claim.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Does not attempt evidence comparison.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Compares evidence with significant prompting and support.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Compares evidence quality with occasional guidance needed.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Independently compares evidence quality across different sides of contested health claims.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Compares evidence quality and explains comparison methodology.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion focuses specifically on the cognitive act of comparing evidence quality, which is distinct from identifying arguments or justifying conclusions.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify the strongest and weakest arguments in a contested health claim debate.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Does not identify argument strengths.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies argument strength with substantial prompting and examples.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Identifies strongest and weakest arguments with some guidance.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Independently identifies strongest and weakest arguments in contested health debates.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Identifies argument strength and explains evaluation criteria used.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion addresses the specific analytical skill of ranking argument strength, which requires different cognitive processes than evidence comparison.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can justify my conclusion about a contested health claim using evidence-based reasoning.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Provides no justification for conclusions.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Attempts justification with significant prompting and structure provided.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Justifies conclusions using evidence with occasional support needed.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Independently justifies conclusions about contested health claims using clear evidence-based reasoning.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Justifies conclusions and acknowledges limitations or uncertainties.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Justification represents a higher-order synthesis skill that builds on but is distinct from the analytical skills of comparing evidence and identifying argument strength.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe one thing I noticed about how I reacted to a situation.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot describe own reaction to situation.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Describes own reaction with prompting and support.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes describes own reaction without prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently describes one specific thing noticed about own reaction to situation.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes reaction and explains why noticing is important.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion focuses on the self-awareness component - noticing and describing one's own reaction to a situation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can say what I might do differently next time.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot suggest different response for next time.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Suggests different response with prompting and guidance.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes suggests what to do differently without prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently suggests specific different response for similar future situations.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Suggests different response and explains why it would be better.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This criterion addresses the forward-thinking adjustment component - proposing alternative responses for future similar situations.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can identify a pattern in how I tend to respond in a type of situation.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot identify response patterns
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Occasionally identifies response patterns with prompting
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes identifies response patterns without prompting
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently identifies response patterns without prompting
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Consistently identifies patterns and can articulate the identification process

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because identifying patterns and explaining reasons are distinct cognitive operations requiring different assessment approaches.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can give a reason for why a response pattern might happen.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: Cannot explain pattern reasons
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Occasionally explains pattern reasons with prompting
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Sometimes explains pattern reasons without prompting
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently explains pattern reasons without prompting
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Consistently explains reasons and can model reasoning for others

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Two criteria are needed because identifying patterns and explaining reasons are distinct cognitive operations requiring different assessment approaches.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can analyse a pattern that I observe across more than one situation in my thinking or behaviour.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No pattern analysis attempted across situations.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Identifies similarities between situations with prompting but limited analysis.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes patterns across situations but analysis lacks depth or clarity.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly analyses patterns across multiple situations, identifying key commonalities and differences in thinking or behaviour.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Analyses patterns thoroughly and can explain the analysis process used.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents the first distinct cognitive operation of analysing patterns across multiple contexts.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can explain what drives the pattern I have identified in my thinking or behaviour.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No explanation of pattern drivers provided.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Attempts to explain drivers with prompting but explanations are unclear.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Provides basic explanations of what drives patterns but may lack insight.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly explains the underlying causes, triggers, or motivations that drive the identified pattern.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Explains pattern drivers with sophisticated insight and can consider multiple influences.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents the second distinct cognitive operation of explaining causal relationships behind observed patterns.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe a specific adjustment I have made or will make to address the pattern.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No specific adjustments described.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Mentions adjustments with prompting but descriptions lack specificity or clarity.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes adjustments but they may be vague or not clearly connected to the pattern.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Describes specific, concrete adjustments that clearly address the identified pattern and its drivers.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes specific adjustments and can explain the reasoning behind the adjustment strategy.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: This represents the third distinct cognitive operation of formulating specific action plans based on pattern analysis.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can evaluate a pattern in my thinking or responses across different contexts.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No pattern evaluation demonstrated
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Evaluates patterns with significant prompting and guidance
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Evaluates patterns sometimes across limited contexts
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Consistently evaluates patterns in thinking or responses across multiple different contexts
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Evaluates patterns comprehensively and explains evaluation approach

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations (evaluate, justify, describe effects) each require separate assessment as they represent different reasoning capabilities.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can justify what sustains a pattern in my thinking or responses.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No justification for sustaining factors
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Attempts justification with prompting but reasoning unclear
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Provides basic justification for some sustaining factors
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly justifies what sustains patterns with sound reasoning and evidence
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Justifies sustaining factors with sophisticated reasoning

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations (evaluate, justify, describe effects) each require separate assessment as they represent different reasoning capabilities.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can describe the effect of a specific adjustment I have made.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: No description of adjustment effects
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: Attempts to describe effects with significant prompting
competency_level_descriptors.developing: Describes some effects of adjustments with basic detail
competency_level_descriptors.competent: Clearly describes specific effects of adjustments made with concrete examples
competency_level_descriptors.extending: Describes effects comprehensively with reflection on impact

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Three distinct cognitive operations (evaluate, justify, describe effects) each require separate assessment as they represent different reasoning capabilities.

criteria.criterion_statement: When a first attempt does not work, the student tries a different approach without needing to be told, and names what happened rather than dismissing it.
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This dispositional learning target represents a unified metacognitive orientation observable through three interconnected behaviours that together demonstrate self-directed learning capacity at this developmental level.
observation_indicators: The student stops and tries a different approach when a first attempt does not work, without needing to be told, on most occasions of minor setback.
Across more than one type of task (reading, making, movement, social), the student shows the same "try differently" response rather than only in one domain.
After a setback, the student names what happened ("I got stuck", "that didn't work because…") rather than dismissing it ("I'm rubbish at this", "it's boring anyway").
The student asks for help once they have tried differently and still need support, not as the first move.
confusable_behaviours: Trying-again as repetition (the same attempt, again) mistaken for trying differently. Distinction: the disposition is a shift in the approach, not only in the effort.
Dismissal framed as humour ("I'm so bad at this lol") mistaken for light-heartedness. Distinction: the disposition shows the student stays with the difficulty rather than exiting it.
absence_indicators: The student exits the task ("I'm rubbish at this" / "this is boring") on first setback across weeks, without the "try differently" move appearing.
The student requires adult prompt to try differently every time, with no internalisation visible across the term.
conversation_prompts: Tell me about a time something didn't work the first time and you tried it a different way. What did you try?
When you're stuck, what do you usually do first?
Tell me about a time a setback was actually helpful — what happened?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student refers back to strategies they have previously found helpful in similar situations without prompt, and volunteers specific observations about their own working rather than general evaluations of the task.
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This dispositional learning target represents a unified orientation toward metacognitive self-direction that manifests through three observable behaviors that work together as indicators of the same underlying disposition.
observation_indicators: The student refers back to a strategy previously found helpful in a similar type of situation, without being prompted ("Last time I did X, so I'll try it again").

After a task, the student volunteers one specific observation about how they worked ("I noticed I kept getting distracted", "it helped when I …"), rather than a general evaluation of the task ("it was fun / hard").
The student notices when they have used a known-helpful strategy and when they have forgotten to, and can name the difference.
confusable_behaviours: General task evaluation ("it was hard / boring / fun") mistaken for self-observation. Distinction: the disposition is about the student's own working, not about the task's quality.
Repeating a strategy because it's the one the class was taught, mistaken for self-directed reuse. Distinction: look for the student's own referring-back language, not lesson-recall.
absence_indicators: The student's post-task reflections stay at the level of task-judgement ("this was easy", "this was boring") over weeks, with no observations about the student's own working surfacing.
The student re-encounters familiar difficulties without re-applying strategies that have previously worked for them.
conversation_prompts: What's a strategy you keep coming back to across different tasks? How did you figure out it was yours?
When do you find it easiest to remember a strategy you already know works, and when do you forget?
Tell me about a specific thing you noticed about how you worked this week.

criteria.criterion_statement: The student identifies patterns in their own responses across situations and names them spontaneously, adjusting approach mid-task when something is not working, without teacher prompt.
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This is a Type 3 dispositional learning target requiring exactly one criterion that captures the observable metacognitive self-direction behaviours at this band level.
observation_indicators: The student identifies a pattern in how they respond across more than one situation and names it spontaneously ("I always do this when the work gets harder"), on multiple occasions across a term.
The student adjusts their approach mid-task in response to noticing that something is not working, without teacher prompt — not only in post-task reflection.
The student's self-observations move from single-domain ("in maths") to cross-domain ("in maths and in music and in chat with my friend") within the term.
confusable_behaviours: Post-hoc pattern-narration (explaining a pattern after the term is over) mistaken for in-the-moment pattern-recognition. Distinction: the disposition is the student catching themselves while the situation is live.
Performance of pattern-language ("I always do X") as classroom currency, with no behavioural adjustment to match. Distinction: the evidence is in the mid-task adjustment, not in the talk.
absence_indicators: The student can reflect on patterns when prompted in reflection lessons but shows no in-the-moment course correction across weeks of observed work.
The student's pattern language stays tightly scoped to one domain (e.g. only maths), not generalising across contexts.
conversation_prompts: What's a pattern in yourself you've caught this term? When did you catch it?
Where do you find it easiest to notice a pattern in real time, and where does it still only land afterwards?
Tell me about a time you changed tack in the middle of something — what made you notice?

criteria.criterion_statement: The student articulates specific adjustments they have made to their thinking or behaviour and describes their effect across more than one domain, applying self-awareness strategies in novel situations without prompt and making unprompted connections between wellbeing learning and other contexts.
criteria.decomposition_rationale: This dispositional learning target represents a unified metacognitive orientation that manifests through interconnected behaviours of self-adjustment, strategy application, and cross-contextual connection-making that cannot be meaningfully separated.
observation_indicators: The student articulates a specific adjustment they have made to their thinking or behaviour and describes its effect across more than one domain (homework and music, sport and social), without prompting.
In novel or challenging situations, the student applies a self-awareness strategy (the pause, naming, body-signal check-in) without adult prompt.
The student makes connections between wellbeing learning and behaviour in other contexts (academic, social, personal) unprompted, on multiple occasions.
The student treats self-observation as an ongoing stance, not a completed assignment.
confusable_behaviours: Articulate reflection without corresponding behaviour change mistaken for dispositional self-direction. Distinction: the disposition shows in cross-domain behavioural shift, not only in fluent reflection.

Technique application in school settings only (uses the pause in class but not in home-context reports) mistaken for dispositional stance. Distinction: the stance carries across observer-free contexts.
absence_indicators: The student's reflections are fluent but adjustments stay siloed to one domain, with no cross-domain pickup reported over a term.
In a genuinely novel challenge, the student reverts to pre-strategy reactivity despite being capable of articulating the strategy when asked.
conversation_prompts: Where does self-direction feel most like 'just how you are' now, and where does it still feel like a technique you're applying?
Tell me about an adjustment you made in one domain that rippled into another — how did that happen?
What's the stance you're taking toward yourself this year — is it different from last year?

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band F as the Do statement represents one integrated systems-level analytical capability using bystander research to propose structural conditions.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed short oral or written task in which I am shown an age-appropriate piece of digital content (a picture, a short video clip, a made-up story), say whether I think it is real or made up, explain my reasoning in a sentence, and name a trusted adult I would ask if I wasn't sure.
criteria.criterion_label: Real versus made-up digital content distinction

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band A.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band A Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band A Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed short oral or written task in which I am shown an age-appropriate piece of digital content (a picture, a short video clip, a made-up story), say whether I think it is real or made up, explain my reasoning in a sentence, and name a trusted adult I would ask if I wasn't sure.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band A Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed short written task, given two pieces of digital content on the same topic, in which I (a) identify the maker of each, (b) state whether each maker is from a "trusted place" and why, and (c) explain why I do or do not believe each piece of content.

competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band B Do statement consistently.

competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band B Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis of a digital claim in which I (a) apply a named verification process (e.g. SIFT) to the claim, (b) identify the origin, the evidence behind it, and the motivation of the source, and (c) justify my separate conclusions about what I would believe, what I would share, and what I would act on.

competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band D Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band D Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis of a digital claim in which I (a) apply a named verification process (e.g. SIFT) to the claim, (b) identify the origin, the evidence behind it, and the motivation of the source, and (c) justify my separate conclusions about what I would believe, what I would share, and what I would act on.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band D Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band D as the Do statement represents one integrated verification-process capability distinguishing believe, share, and act as three separate decisions.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written evaluation of a contested claim in which I (a) weigh evidence across multiple sources using named criteria, (b) identify at least two specific algorithmic, platform, or production-incentive factors that shaped how the claim reached me, (c) name the self-bias most likely to affect my interpretation (confirmation, motivated reasoning, in-group alignment), and (d) justify my conclusion with explicit awareness of that bias.
criteria.criterion_label: Algorithmic context and self-bias aware evaluation

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band E Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band E Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed written evaluation of a contested claim in which I (a) weigh evidence across multiple sources using named criteria, (b) identify at least two specific algorithmic, platform, or production-incentive factors that shaped how the claim reached me, (c) name the self-bias most likely to affect my interpretation (confirmation, motivated reasoning, in-group alignment), and (d) justify my conclusion with explicit awareness of that bias.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band E Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band E as the Do statement represents one integrated capability extending evaluation to platform incentives and self-bias awareness.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis of a real-world contested claim involving synthetic, AI-generated, or sophisticated disinformation, in which I (a) apply detection and verification methods appropriate to the claim, (b) compare my verification process against a recognised critical-appraisal framework (e.g. SIFT, Wineburg's professional fact-checker heuristics), and (c) construct a reasoned conclusion that states what I believe, what I would share, what I would act on, and — specifically — what I cannot know and why.
criteria.criterion_label: Synthetic disinformation detection and epistemic humility

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band F Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band F Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis of a real-world contested claim involving synthetic, AI-generated, or sophisticated disinformation, in which I (a) apply detection and verification methods appropriate to the claim, (b) compare my verification process against a recognised critical-appraisal framework (e.g. SIFT, Wineburg's professional fact-checker heuristics), and (c) construct a reasoned conclusion that states what I believe, what I would share, what I would act on, and — specifically — what I cannot know and why.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band F Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band F as the Do statement represents one integrated mature verification capability including meta-evaluation against professional standards and explicit epistemic humility.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed short written analysis (e.g. 200–300 words) of a chosen digital product in which I (a) identify one specific persuasive-design feature using correct vocabulary, (b) describe one reason (drawing on dopamine / reward / habit-loop mechanism) the design works on people, and (c) explain one specific effect I have noticed on myself (time, mood, behaviour).
criteria.criterion_label: Persuasive design feature identification and effect

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band C.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band C Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band C Do statement consistently.

competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band C Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band C as the Do statement represents the baseline integrated capability of identifying persuasive design and connecting mechanism to personal effect. LT 8.2 Band C+ is a conceptual accelerator for LT 8.3 (Digital Assertiveness & Wellbeing Strategies), which will be added in the next session — no edges to LT 8.3 are added here pending that session.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis of a chosen digital product using the "attention / emotion / behaviour" framework in which I (a) identify at least two interacting persuasive-design features using correct vocabulary, (b) analyse how each shapes attention, emotion, or behaviour in the product, and (c) explain one specific effect I have noticed on my own thinking, mood, or time.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band D.

competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band D Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.decomposition_rationale: Single criterion for Band D as the Do statement represents one integrated analytical capability using the attention/emotion/behaviour framework across multiple design features. LT 8.2 Band D is a conceptual accelerator for LT 8.3 — no edges added pending next session.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written evaluation of a chosen digital platform in which I (a) evaluate how the platform algorithmically curates what I see (with evidence from my own observation or documentation), (b) explain at least two specific psychological or economic incentives driving that design decision, and (c) justify one specific change I will make to my use of the platform based on my analysis — including why that change addresses the mechanism, not just the symptom.

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band E.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band E Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band E Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed written evaluation of a chosen digital platform in which I (a) evaluate how the platform algorithmically curates what I see (with evidence from my own observation or documentation), (b) explain at least two specific psychological or economic incentives driving that design decision, and (c) justify one specific change I will make to my use of the platform based on my analysis — including why that change addresses the mechanism, not just the symptom.
competency_level_descriptors.extending: The student demonstrates the Band E Do statement and additionally transfers the capability to an unfamiliar context, teaches or explains it to others, or demonstrates unusual depth beyond the band expectation.

criteria.criterion_statement: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis that (a) evaluates the cumulative effect of persuasive design across a sustained period on my attention, relationships, and identity — drawing on my own evidence (e.g. tracked use, tracked mood, observation), (b) compares my analysis against at least two recognised pieces of research on digital wellbeing, including at least one that contests a claim I find sympathetic, and (c) articulates a reasoned personal position specifying where I will and will not allow these systems to operate on me, acknowledging what the evidence supports strongly, weakly, and where it remains genuinely uncertain.
criteria.criterion_label: Cumulative persuasive design impact and values-based stance

competency_level_descriptors.no_evidence: The student has not produced work that demonstrates any aspect of this learning target at Band F.
competency_level_descriptors.emerging: The student shows initial engagement with the Band F Do statement but requires significant support or prompting.
competency_level_descriptors.developing: The student demonstrates some but not all components of the Band F Do statement consistently.
competency_level_descriptors.competent: I can complete a rubric-assessed written analysis that (a) evaluates the cumulative effect of persuasive design across a sustained period on my attention, relationships, and identity — drawing on my own evidence (e.g. tracked use, tracked mood, observation), (b) compares my analysis against at least two recognised pieces of research on digital wellbeing, including at least one that contests a claim I find sympathetic, and (c) articulates a reasoned personal position specifying where I will and will not allow these systems to operate on me, acknowledging what the evidence supports strongly, weakly, and where it remains genuinely uncertain.
